_comment: Million-row head-to-head: pgvector HNSW vs pg_turbovec. See docs/RECALL.md § 2.1.0 for narrative.
fixture: synthetic random vectors (random()-0.5)*2 then l2-normalised, seed=0.42

ground_truth.method: exact brute-force seq scan on FP32

notes: Random unit vectors in 384-d have no clustering structure: HNSW's graph has no useful local neighbourhood signal, so even ef_search=200 only finds 11.6% of the true top-10. This is *the* well-known pessimistic case for graph indexes; on real-world embeddings (see GloVe-100 numbers in docs/RECALL.md § 2.1.1) HNSW gets 80-90% R@10.
pg_turbovec scans every quantised row (no graph, no buckets), so its recall is independent of the data distribution. R@10 = 1.0 at 4-bit, 0.92-0.97 at 2-bit.

Per-query latency for pg_turbovec is dominated by the per-scan deserialisation of the IdMapIndex from turbovec.am_storage (~195 MB at 4-bit, ~103 MB at 2-bit) via tmpfile. The cache in src/cache.rs exists but is NOT wired into the index AM scan path in v1.0; every amgettuple invocation pays the full deserialise cost. This is the dominant cost in the table above; the kernel itself is sub-second for 1M × 384 at 4-bit.
search_k = 100 vs 200 has small effect: it caps how many candidates the index returns to the executor, so it changes heap re-rank cost but not the kernel scan or deserialise cost.

tv4_k100 has an anomalous max (27.5 s) and p95 (24.8 s); tv4_k200 in the same session has tight p95 (7.1 s). Most likely an early-run effect (bgwriter / autovacuum activity); the bench did a 2-call warmup but warmup only primes PG buffer cache, not the per-scan deserialise.
